meta.title: AWS AppSync Real-Time Serverless Web App
meta.description: A movie voting app using AppSync, Lambda, Step Functions, and DynamoDB. CloudWatch Events kick off a scheduled workflow that rotates the current movie every 60 seconds. All connected clients receive real-time updates via GraphQL subscriptions over WebSocket.
Browsers and mobile apps. Hold a persistent GraphQL subscription (WebSocket) to AppSync for real-time movie and vote updates.
Fully serverless. Lambda, AppSync, Step Functions, and DynamoDB all scale to zero.
Scheduled pipeline: CloudWatch fires every 60 s → Step Functions loops → Lambda invoked every 10 s.
DynamoDB tables for movie metadata and vote counts. Local Resolver for ephemeral chat pub/sub (no persistence).
Third-party REST API (themoviedb.org). Lambda calls GET /movie/popular to fetch metadata for a random top-50 movie.
Browsers and mobile apps running the voting UI. Maintain a persistent GraphQL subscription over WebSocket to receive real-time movie poster, synopsis, and leaderboard updates.
meta.notes: Subscriptions auto-reconnect. Max concurrent connections: 1 000 000 (AppSync limit).

meta.description: Scheduled EventBridge rule fires every 60 seconds and triggers the Step Functions state machine.
Standard workflow state machine. Loops up to 6 times per 60-second window, invoking Lambda every 10 seconds. Each iteration picks a new movie.
meta.notes: States: Wait(10 s) → InvokeLambda → Choice(iterations < 6) → loop or End.

meta.description: Core backend function. Fetches a random popular movie from The Movie DB API, sends GraphQL mutations to AppSync to update the current movie and zero votes, and handles vote processing via Lambda resolver.

Managed GraphQL API. Handles mutations (updateMovie, castVote, sendMessage), resolves queries, and broadcasts subscription events to all connected clients over WebSocket. Local Resolver used for ephemeral chat.
meta.notes: Data sources: AMAZON_DYNAMODB (Movies, Votes), AWS_LAMBDA (vote resolver), LOCAL (chat).

meta.description: Stores the currently active movie: id, title, synopsis, posterUrl. A single item overwritten on each rotation.
meta.notes: On-demand capacity. Table: Movies. PK: movieId (always 'CURRENT').

meta.description: Atomic counter table for vote tallies per movie. Zeroed on each rotation; incremented on every castVote mutation.
meta.notes: UpdateItem with ADD #votes 1 for atomic increment. Table: Votes. PK: movieId.

meta.description: AppSync LOCAL data source for the public chatroom. Routes sendMessage mutations directly to onMessage subscription triggers with no Lambda invocation and no DynamoDB write — messages are ephemeral.
meta.notes: sendMessage mutation → LOCAL → onMessage subscription. Not persisted.

meta.description: Third-party REST API (api.themoviedb.org/3). Lambda calls GET /movie/popular, picks a random entry from the top-50 list, and retrieves full metadata.
meta.notes: Bearer token auth. Rate limit: 40 req/10 s.

steps.name: Overview
steps.title: AppSync Real-Time Architecture Overview
steps.description: A serverless movie voting app. CloudWatch Events trigger a Step Functions workflow every 60 seconds. Lambda fetches a new movie, pushes it to AppSync, which broadcasts to all subscribed clients in real time. Clients vote and chat via GraphQL mutations.

steps.name: CloudWatch fires
steps.title: ① CloudWatch Events initiates Step Functions
steps.description: An Amazon EventBridge (CloudWatch Events) scheduled rule fires every 60 seconds. It starts a new execution of the Step Functions state machine, which will orchestrate the next round of movie rotation.

steps.name: Step Functions → Lambda
steps.title: ② Step Functions triggers Lambda every 10 seconds
steps.description: The state machine loops up to 6 times per 60-second window. Each iteration waits 10 seconds then invokes Lambda. This gives clients a new movie to vote on every 10 seconds.

annotations.text: Wait(10 s) → Task: InvokeLambda → Choice: iteration < 6?

steps.name: Fetch movie
steps.title: ③ Lambda fetches a random movie from The Movie DB
steps.description: Lambda calls the third-party Movie DB REST API to retrieve the current popular movies list, picks a random entry from the top 50, and fetches full metadata (title, synopsis, poster URL).

steps.name: Lambda → AppSync
steps.title: ④ Lambda sends GraphQL mutations to AppSync
steps.description: Lambda sends two GraphQL mutations to AppSync: updateCurrentMovie (sets the new movie) and resetVotes (zeroes all vote counters). AppSync resolves these against the DynamoDB data sources.

data.query: mutation UpdateCurrentMovie($input: MovieInput!) { updateCurrentMovie(input: $input) { id title posterUrl } }

steps.name: AppSync → DynamoDB
steps.title: ⑤ AppSync writes to Movies and Votes tables
steps.description: AppSync resolves the mutations against its DynamoDB data sources: the Movies table is updated with the new movie, and the Votes table counters are zeroed so clients start fresh for this round.

steps.name: Broadcast to clients
steps.title: ⑥ All clients receive the new movie in real time
steps.description: Because clients are subscribed to the onUpdateCurrentMovie subscription, AppSync automatically broadcasts the mutation result to every connected WebSocket connection. All clients simultaneously see the new movie poster and synopsis.

annotations.text: subscription.onUpdateCurrentMovie → broadcast to all WebSocket connections

steps.name: Clients vote
steps.title: ⑦ Clients vote and chat during the 10-second window
steps.description: During the 10-second voting window, clients send castVote mutations to AppSync, which increments the atomic counter in the Votes table. Clients also send sendMessage mutations for the public chatroom.

steps.name: Leaderboard update
steps.title: ⑧ Lambda updates the leaderboard via AppSync
steps.description: When a castVote mutation arrives, AppSync invokes the Lambda resolver for vote processing. Lambda reads the updated tally from DynamoDB and pushes a leaderboard mutation back through AppSync, which broadcasts the updated score to all subscribers.

steps.name: Chat (ephemeral)
steps.title: ⑨ Chat messages via Local Resolver — no back-end storage
steps.description: When a client sends a sendMessage mutation, AppSync processes it through a LOCAL data source resolver — no Lambda invocation, no DynamoDB write. The message is immediately broadcast to all onMessage subscribers and then discarded. Only clients connected at that moment receive it.

annotations.text: sendMessage → LOCAL resolver → onMessage subscription trigger (no PutItem)
